message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    COVID-19 has become a major public health problem. There is good evidence that ACE2 is a receptor for SARS-CoV-2, and high expression of
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    may increase susceptibility to infection. We aimed to explore risk factors affecting susceptibility to infection and prioritize drug repositioning candidates, based on Mendelian randomization (MR) studies on
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    lung expression.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We conducted a phenome-wide MR study to prioritize diseases/traits and blood proteins causally linked to
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    lung expression in GTEx. We also explored drug candidates whose targets overlapped with the top-ranked proteins in MR, as these drugs may alter
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression and may be clinically relevant.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The most consistent finding was tentative evidence of an association between diabetes-related traits and increased
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression. Based on one of the largest GWAS on type 2 diabetes (T2DM) to date (
                    <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                    =898,130), T2DM was causally linked to raised
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression(p=2.91E-03;MR-IVW). Significant associations(at nominal level;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    <0.05) with
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression was observed across multiple DM datasets and analytic methods, for type 1 and 2 diabetes and related traits including early start of insulin. Other diseases/traits having nominal significant associations with increased expression included inflammatory bowel disease, (ER+)breast and lung cancers, asthma, smoking and elevated ALT. We also identified drugs that may target the top-ranked proteins in MR, such as fostamatinib and zinc.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Our analysis suggested that diabetes and related traits may increase
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression, which may influence susceptibility to infection (or more severe infection). However, none of these findings withstood rigorous multiple testing corrections (at FDR<0.05). Proteome-wide MR analyses might help uncover mechanisms underlying
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression and guide drug repositioning. Further studies are required to verify our findings.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Exploring diseases/traits and blood proteins causally related to expression of ACE2, the putative receptor of SARS-CoV-2: A Mendelian Randomization analysis highlights tentative relevance of diabetes-related traits
